mnemonics: CIA (Confidence Intervals & Assumptions) - For large samples, you can be confident your interval is accurate if you assume normality.
keyPoints: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) allows us to use z-tests and normal-based confidence intervals for the difference between two means, even if the underlying population distributions are not normal.
The standard error for the difference between two independent sample means is calculated as: sqrt[(s1²/n1) + (s2²/n2)].
The primary goal is often to test the null hypothesis (H₀: μ₁ = μ₂) against an alternative hypothesis (H₁: μ₁ ≠ μ₂, H₁: μ₁ > μ₂, or H₁: μ₁ < μ₂).
commonMistakes: Using a z-test (for large known σ) or a normal distribution when a t-test (for estimated σ) is more appropriate for smaller samples, confusing the sample size requirements.
Failing to check the assumption of independence between the two samples before performing the test, which invalidates the procedure.
Incorrectly pooling variances or calculating the standard error when the samples are independent, mistakenly using a formula for paired data.
